intent: A relabeled SSH host key: sshd_t denied read+open on a default_t file (a host key file that lost its sshd_key_t label, e.g. moved/restored from a backup). Maps to one clean narrow allow; audit2allow -R substitutes the broad files_read_default_files() interface.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic).

oracle.narrower_than_R_by: -R substitutes the files_read_default_files() interface macro, which grants read+open+getattr+ioctl+lock (read_file_perms padding) on ALL default_t files plus list/getattr on default_t dirs - far broader than the denied { read open } on the single file class. Our expected.te emits exactly `allow sshd_t default_t:file { read open };` - the 2 denied perms, no macro, no perm padding, no dir grant.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive impl mirroring the -R default would emit files_read_default_files(sshd_t), granting sshd read of EVERY default_t file (and dirs) system-wide instead of the one denied access. For a security-sensitive daemon like sshd this is exactly the over-broadening triage exists to refuse. The real fix is also a relabel (restorecon the host key to sshd_key_t).
